content:
Problems with Colibri engine?

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Marc Newberry on UnsplashWhen Colibri went viral, one headline grabbed everyone’s attention:
“Run GLM 5.2 (744B) on just 25 GB of RAM.”
At first glance, it sounds revolutionary. A frontier-scale language model running on consumer hardware without requiring hundreds of gigabytes of memory or expensive GPUs.
But after digging into the GitHub repository, benchmarks, and architecture, I don’t think Colibri is the practical breakthrough many people believe it is.Technically, it’s an impressive engineering project. Practically, for most users, it’s a waste.
Let’s understand why.

The 25 GB RAM Claim is Misleading
The biggest marketing point of Colibri is that it can run GLM 5.2 using only around 25 GB of RAM. That statement is technically correct.However, RAM is only one part of the story.
You still need around 370 GB of local NVMe storage just for the converted model. During conversion, even more temporary storage is required. If you don’t own a large, fast SSD, you simply cannot run the model.
So while memory usage looks impressive, the storage requirements remain enormous.

Performance is Extremely Slow
The repository openly admits the limitations.

• On a cold cache, each generated token may require reading nearly 11 GB of expert weights from disk.

• Even with speculation and caching, many benchmark systems still generate well below 1 token per second.
Some optimized machines reach 1–2 tokens per second, while large multi-GPU systems can exceed that. But those machines have hundreds of gigabytes of RAM, multiple RTX 5090 GPUs, and carefully tuned caches.
For the average developer, the experience is nowhere close to using ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Waiting several seconds for every token quickly becomes frustrating.

It Gets Faster Only After “Learning”
Colibri relies heavily on caching.As you continue using similar prompts, it records frequently accessed experts and pins them into RAM or VRAM. This means benchmark numbers improve only after multiple sessions.
If your workload constantly changes, for example switching between coding, writing, reasoning, mathematics, and multilingual tasks the cache becomes much less effective.
Real-world workloads are rarely repetitive enough to benefit consistently.

It Isn’t Built for Production
Colibri exposes an OpenAI-compatible API, but underneath it still processes only one generation at a time.
Modern inference engines like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and SGLang maximize GPU utilization through continuous batching and parallel request processing.Colibri intentionally avoids this architecture.
As a result, it isn’t suitable for serving multiple users simultaneously.

Storage is the Real Bottleneck
The project treats SSDs as an extension of memory.
That idea is clever.Unfortunately, storage latency is still thousands of times slower than RAM.Every cache miss means waiting for another expert to be loaded from disk.Even PCIe Gen5 SSDs cannot eliminate this bottleneck.
The result is an inference engine whose speed depends more on your storage subsystem than your processor.

Quality Evaluation is Limited
The repository focuses heavily on engineering achievements.However, model quality evaluation is relatively limited.The published benchmarks cover only a small number of questions from datasets like MMLU, ARC, and HellaSwag.
There are no extensive comparisons on coding benchmarks, reasoning tasks, long-context performance, or agent evaluations.
Without broader testing, it’s difficult to know how much aggressive quantization affects real-world performance.

Complex Setup for Limited Benefits
Unlike tools such as Ollama or LM Studio, Colibri requires users to understand concepts like:
RAM budgets
Expert pinning
Cache tuning
Speculative decoding
CUDA tiers
Routing parameters
Storage hierarchy
The learning curve is significantly higher, while the practical performance gains remain limited for most desktop users.

Is Colibri Bad?
Absolutely not.
From an engineering perspective, Colibri is one of the most innovative open-source AI projects released this year.Streaming MoE experts directly from disk while preserving routing behavior is a remarkable technical achievement.
The developers have solved problems that many believed were impossible only a year ago.
The issue is not the engineering.
The issue is the expectations.
Many people see “744B model on 25 GB RAM” and assume they will get ChatGPT-level responsiveness on their laptop.
That simply isn’t the case.

Final Thoughts
Colibri proves something important: running frontier-scale Mixture-of-Experts models on consumer hardware is possible.
But “possible” doesn’t always mean “practical.” For researchers interested in systems engineering, memory hierarchies, and MoE inference, Colibri is fascinating.
For developers looking for a fast local assistant, simpler solutions like smaller 30B–70B models running entirely in RAM or VRAM will usually provide a much better experience.
In the end, Colibri is less about replacing existing inference engines and more about demonstrating what is technically achievable. It’s an impressive proof of concept but for most users today, the trade-offs in storage, speed, and complexity outweigh the headline advantage of running a 744B model on just 25 GB of RAM.
